Hi. I remember way back in that I dated a girl. It lasted for eight months, on and off. The relationship completely ended in . I'm a girl also, and a fully confirmed bisexual. How are you, lass? I'll use that term because you were once the greatest girl I ever met even if it was online. Even if I never met who you really are. I really loved you but we were both toxic with each other. The constant stress at my strict parents finding out about us, and you, constantly flirting with the other users in the chats. I once loved you but it doesn't mean that I will deadass talk shit about you. You were once my person, I do respect that. We were once lovers, even if it's only for eight months. That timeline of eight months makes it the greatest time of my life. We met because of a game, and because of an Amino app where I decided to interact with the other people who would play that game. And then I met you. I fully moved on. Thank you for making my eight months a happy timeline of my life. I'm now in a relationship for six years with my current boyfriend, and he provides peace and happiness. The constant assurance despite how busy we both are. His mere presence makes me sleepy because he makes me feel safe. To my boyfriend, baby. I love you, my baby. Thank you for staying despite my chaotic personality, your stable presence made me realize how toxic I am that I changed, and decided to do it for you. Couple rings, shared snacks, etc. I'd love to do it everyday with you because time with you becomes the best time of my life.

The experience at the campus Fitness Center has significantly declined. While it was once a fantastic, free, and convenient place to train, the atmosphere has completely shifted since the change in HKP head. We understand the necessity of rules, but the current head's methods are a bit unacceptable; my friends and I have suffered multiple bad experiences, including being subjected to public shaming. Now, many of us are forced to pay for external gyms, which offer the flexibility our center lacks: they have lenient rules regarding attire, are Tech-Friendly (allowing usage of phones while you rest - nagbbrowse [REDACTED_NAME] nagpapahinga nacall-out na agad), and let you work out at your own pace without the constant pressure of a time limit. Furthermore, the new limited number system at the fitness center makes access a headache (parang lugi ka pag naunahan ka, [REDACTED_NAME] studyante [REDACTED_NAME], around 10-15 [REDACTED_NAME]). The truth is that the "free and accessible" option is no longer worth the trouble, and we sincerely hope the center can be restored to a supportive, friendly space for all students.

I seriously don’t think some people or students in UP understand the gravity of earthquakes in Baguio City, even if they’re “mild” or “quick.” People from Baguio are still traumatized from the earthquake. That disaster didn’t just shake the city. It killed families, destroyed homes, and left the city isolated because roads were damaged or buried. Even the thought of aftershocks scares most people. This is so goddamn pathetic dammit, not to selfishly make such a scary event revolve around myself, but i think the earthquake made me realize that im one bad day away from ending it all. When i felt the shaking happen, it didnt really occur to me that i should flee. I just kinda sat in my desk, panicking because I still havent done a requirement that was due later that day. A few moments later, I got scared, because the possibility of me actually dying was becoming reality with every minute I just sat there doing nothing. And then suddenly, numbness. Okay, so what if I'm going to die? Atleast I dont have to go study for an exam next week, nor will I have to think about the countless reports that I have to write. The ground below me could have fallen away, and my last thought would have been na "[REDACTED_NAME] [REDACTED_NAME], [REDACTED_NAME] nila bank details [REDACTED_NAME]?" I guess I've subconsciously known all this time na I've just been passively living, not actively trying but already alright with the prospect if I were to suddenly die. When I walk to school, I pray that a car would hit me as I cross the road. Or when I'm sleeping, I hope that I strangle myself by accident. Because I dont want my family and friends to think that their efforts to love me werent enough to keep me going, I want it to be an accident so that (hopefully) no one could be blamed for it but myself. Tatanga [REDACTED_NAME], hindi tumitingin pag naglalakad. Bobong anak, nakadikit [REDACTED_NAME] [REDACTED_NAME]. I'm just so, so sick of everything. I get it, I have no one else to blame for it but myself. I'm the one thinking these thoughts, I'm the one with the shitty discipline, Im the one with the goddawful priorities. Ngayon kesa tapusin [REDACTED_NAME], humahagulgol. [REDACTED_NAME], ang [REDACTED_NAME], ginagastos mo pa oras mo sa walang [REDACTED_NAME]. Nakakapikon na talaga. Blatant bullying coming out from this page is crazy. Especially when your featured post literally highlights your "rules" about refraining to post such submissions. Knowing this social era, there are probably more bullying submissions being entered everyday towards average normal people, the nameless and such. And yet, targeted ones, especially towards people holding roles in certain student body organizations, tend to be the ones posted. It says something about some subconscious agenda this page is forwarding, and maybe even personal coming from the moderators. This is not to invalidate the feelings and concerns of students in holding people accountable for certain actions and inactions. But when the line is crossed and that call for accountability becomes public humiliation, it risks this slippery slope to just plainly attack certain individuals, like mocking them or even making fun of their physical appearance. It lessens the incentives for a better approach in having solvency and it sensationalizes bullying. Let us remember that the people targeted are also students within this school, or even that they are also human. They absolutely need to apologize or at the very least come out with a statement regarding the issues of their action, however, to call for with bullying wouldn't make haste for it to happen nor would it make the situation any better. It only demoralizes the call and paints it as an excuse to spread such negativity. For the admins of this page, do better. For the students who are being criticized and rightly so, hear the student body for their call for an apology.

This is a message for someone who's studying in [REDACTED_NAME] (I'm not from UP). I won't drop anyone's name or any information about them but I want to let then know something, and for this message I'll just use the name "flower". Dear, Flower Hello, first of all I'd like to let you know tgat I'm doing well, I just finished my tasks for my chemistry subject that I've been procrastinating on for so long (lol). I just want to extend a message that will probably never reach you since I know that your schedule is very hectic and busy. But, through this freedom wall I want to try—because you'll never know until you don't try right? So even if the chance of you seeing this message or even reading it is unlikely I still want to tell you that I wish things didn't end so abruptly. Yes, you were honest with me and I really appreciate it—I do, you were also the only person who was considerate enough to explain things to me instead of just leaving me hanging by a thread. I understand why you said the things that you said [REDACTED_NAME] I want to be selfish with what I am about to say—why did it have to be this way? I know that I'm not supposed to ask but right now I think I'm turning to the most irrational person in the world. I only met you a few days ago, I was really looking forward to what this encounter had in store for me—for us? Right now I can't even find the right words to say. What's also crushing to me is how I was supposed to show you something that I had made (a simole artwork that's not necessarily a portrait of you hut just an artwork in general it was a doodle I made on school) because we both shared this love for art, and just as I was about to show you we had THAT conversation, and my heart suddenly started to sink. Do you know that I desperately tried to not say the word "please" while we were having that conversation because I didn't want to seem like I was begging especially since we just met, but I really can't shake it off right now. I wanted to keep what we had—kahit friends [REDACTED_NAME] and see if it can be taken to another place. I was all over the place when we were taking and I couldn't find the words to say and even now—in this freedom wall I think I'm starting to sound like a brokem record because I don't even know if this message makes sense. This is my last resort, this is my only option now and it's not even guaranteed to reach you. You made me feel like I was finally being considered for. How I might feel, how I might react. And in my eyes to me [REDACTED_NAME] you already had reciprocated my efforts. But I just couldn't tell you that on the spot when we had that conversation. When you also decided to end things, you were so transparent and kind with it and that makes it so harder for me to forget about it. I wish you could have lied and just told me that you hated me so that I could move forward easier—but no, you were so gentle with me, and that in itself made me feel like I was being treated with care. Yes, it's basic human decency but it's how I really felt at that time. Everyday I woke up I looked forward to talking with you even if our conversations were short, it gave me so much to look forward to, but because of your empathy and kindness you decided that to me the effort that you gave by replying even if you're tired, body being heavy and all, and eyes dropping was not enough—it was more than enough to me. I didn't want to be imposing on you during our conversation but I wanted to ask so bad that "are you sure you are not self-sabotaging" I didn't want tk ask that because it seemed offensive and rude and out of the line but what if that were the case? I was happy naman with what we had kahit na di pa tayo everyday mag usap eh. You told me that I deserved someone who's going to meet me in the middle pero to me—from my perception you already did. I was holding back myself from saying the most desperate and stupid things that time pero right now I just can't do this anymore. This is probably going to be my last message for you, flower. and you might not even see it I pray and hope that you become successful, flower. Take care, and I hope that before I forget about you, we cross paths—because I don't want to forget about you, I still want to hold onto it. Your's truly, Corv

As a student, I understand that learning is a shared responsibility. It’s a collaboration between both the professor and the students’ efforts. While our personal effort is essential, I truly believe that the way lessons are delivered plays a crucial role in how well we’re able to grasp complex topics. When only a few students manage to pass our long exam, it might be a sign that there’s room for adjustment, not just on the professors side and not just on students side, but maybe on BOTH sides. We understand that teaching is not an easy job, and we truly respect that. But I also think that being a good instructor means being open to feedback, adjusting when needed, and continuing to grow. Just like we students are expected to improve, professors can also reflect and adapt. This isn’t about pointing fingers or blaming anyone. I just hope we can find ways to better adapt to each other’s needs for the sake of a better learning experience for all of us :))

I dont really post here pero quick rant [REDACTED_NAME]. I got the things I wanted—just not the way I imagined-[REDACTED_NAME] R. Growing up, I always believed that UP was the dream. I worked relentlessly, made sacrifices, and poured so much of myself into getting here. But now, I’m beginning to realize that the reality is far more difficult than I ever anticipated.I knew the standards would be high. I expected the workload to be heavy. But I didn’t expect the toll it would take on my mental health.I’ve been chasing perfect scores, or at least decent grades so much that I’ve stopped celebrating my wins. When I succeed, I don’t even feel happy ,I just feel relief that I didn’t fail. I know how many hours I’ve spent on every assignment, exam, and project. And yet, when I fall short of my goals, I spiral. I feel like I’ve failed not just academically, but as a person. Like I’ve jeopardized my chance at Latin honors. Like I’ve let myself down.The first time I was named a University Scholar, I was proud. I felt seen. But every recognition after that felt less like an achievement and more like a responsibility. I’m grateful to those who remind me that my grades don’t define me. I hear them. I appreciate them. But deep down, I struggle to believe it. Because right now, my grades feel like the only thing holding me together.I often picture myself standing on a pedestal; one wrong move, one misstep, and everything I’ve built could come crashing down.

As a member of the IP community, I didn't really perceive the hyping to be disrespectful because even in our tribe we also do the same thing. Community dances are always part of any occasion here in our province. As someone who took part in these dances, yes they are fun and lively with some hyping involved here and then. There is a clear distinction between community dances and formal traditional dances, the latter being the one that PAGTA performed first. In those kind of dances, it is indeed important to be quiet and pay attention as a form of respect. Hopefully, when we witness these dances in the future we will practice these actions. Since this issue has sparked discussions about Cordillera culture, I would like to talk more about the deeper issues here. I saw someone's post saying that those from the lowlands should learn the culture of the Cordillerans since they are now residing in our land. I wholeheartedly agree with this. It baffles me that some seniors in their 4th year still cannot speak or even understand [REDACTED_NAME]. Given that you have resided here for four years, how come you still cannot communicate even in basic [REDACTED_NAME]? If you are to reside here for four years, I believe it is one's responsibility to atleast educate themselves regarding the culture and language of our Igorot people. I hope that our freshies now would take this initiative. There's no rush and pressure here but a little bit of initiative and effort would be greatly appreciated. It is a sign of basic respect to at least inform ourselves of basic etiquette and traditions given that you will be interacting with the IP community for four years. I also think it's really sad to rarely see people who speak [REDACTED_NAME] here. I myself who has been limited to speak in Tagalog feel like I haven't been able to express myself freely because I have used our language my whole life. I really hope that in the future, our [CAR] community will be more used to using the [REDACTED_NAME] language in day-to-day communication and even in our panawagans for our rallies. To our freshies, I hope this issue will not discourage you from engaging with IP cultural practices and learning about our culture. To my fellow IPs, I hope that our community will grow even more as the years go by. More power to the Cordillera!

It’s sad how PAGTA is the one defending the freshies against the [CAR] community, saying na hindi nirespeto ang PAGTA. Shouldn’t PAGTA be the one telling us what’s offensive and what’s not? Anyway, sa kapupuna niyo sa crowd reaction, we didn’t even think about why they reacted that way. PAGTA, U WERE ON FIRE! So this is really an appreciation post for you. The song was such a good mood-setter. I really appreciated the use of bamboo instruments, paired with the amazing songs and singers. The BANGA! Girl, that was so amazing. Notice how there were two sets of pairs dancing the pair dance. The first pair wasn’t cheered on with “kiss! kiss!” You know why? Because they weren’t a couple. But they set the mood for the 2nd pair. The second pair had so much chemistry. That’s where the loud cheers and “kiss! kiss!” chanting started. And I know why—[REDACTED_NAME] they’re a real thing. Mag-jowa sila. [REDACTED_NAME]. The community dance was chaotic, but PAGTA still tried their best to include everyone. And honestly, hindi [REDACTED_NAME] dances [REDACTED_NAME] “lower! lower! lower!” In fact, parang naging [CAR] tradition na siya every time PAGTA does a community dance. Was anyone offended before? [REDACTED_NAME]. Because everybody knew how normal the cheering was. This doesn’t mean [REDACTED_NAME] culture or [REDACTED_NAME]. It just means engaging yung performance [REDACTED_NAME]. It would only be disrespectful if the performers themselves were being laughed at or if the dance steps were being mocked. But that’s not what happened—the crowd was simply carried away by the mood. No one aimed to disrespect anyone or anyone’s culture. Let people have fun. There’s more to life than hatred.

HAHA! At first, I thought it was just another freedom wall post that people would scroll past, but when I checked it again this morning, it had already gained a lot of attention. Personally, I don’t think there was any disrespect intended during last Freshie Night. The same kinds of performances happened last year, and even the year before that, and no one really took offense back then. Of course, I’m not saying that everything should stay “normalized” without question, but maybe we should also avoid immediately interpreting things as offensive when that isn’t the intent. For example, in courtship dances we even shout “[REDACTED_NAME]” or tease with playful sounds like “yiee,” and those have never really been taken as offensive. We know. I know. It is also our culture and experience. Someone even mentioned that we should also inquire our Anthropology Professors and for the years we’ve seen this name and organization in [CAR] events, I believe at least one of them should’ve already commented about it and talked to them about it. Anyway, to the freshies—welcome to [CAR]! I hope you find your purpose here in Baguio, or here mismo sa [CAR], by immersing yourself in our culture. That same culture will also shape you throughout your stay in the Cordillera. This post is not in any way, shape or form affiliated with PAGTA, alumni, and the current members of the organization. Since there’s an upcoming walkout again, I would like to leave a reminder to everyone. It’s okay to urge your fellow students to come out of their classrooms, but please be mindful in doing so. Last year, may nakabasag [REDACTED_NAME] window [REDACTED_NAME] because they wanted their fellow students to walk out (I think one of the USC councilors na itong nakabasag, so may they reach out to those na nag-organize para safe ang lahat). [REDACTED_NAME] at walang natamaan o nagalusan ng shards that time. So, again, please be mindful. Do not force students out, let them walk out of their classrooms. Hindi po ito “force out,” kundi walkout. You remove the essence of a walkout if you are forcing students out of their classes. Also, do understand that some students do not have the privilege to walk out of their classes (due to sanctions). And yes, I am aware that walking out of class means sacrifice or compromise, but some professors/instructors are still conservative/counter-progressive to the core (yes, they still exist, even in UP). Last year, I know people who were held by their instructors inside the classroom and were told off, especially if they had a placard in plain sight. So, please, in the spirit of protest and activism, let's be understanding sa isa't isa.

I didn't appreciate what I saw earlier. Found out that one of my professors had only graduated last year. Out of curiosity and a need for motivation as a freshie, I looked through his social media. I wanted to see how someone ahead of me had managed this difficult course. I added him on Facebook, and he accepted. I followed his Instagram and was glad to see his many interests. Then I came across a story that unsettled me. He was complaining about the weight of his work while pursuing his master’s degree. Instead of encouragement, I felt disappointment. As a freshie, I wanted to look up to him. Yet what I saw was disheartening. Not everyone is given the chances he now has, and many would sacrifice much to be in his place. Privilege is not in having the position. Honor is in how you carry it.

To the girl my ex cheated on me with. All my life I've always just wanted to live a normal and private life, everyone who knows me would know that. Everything I've ever had, I had to honor and cherish in comfort and privacy, and that includes him. I'm grown enough to understand that I couldn't have done anything differently to change what has happened. I know that it was never anything about me that made him do what he did. Breakups happen all the time, and some happens because it was always meant to. I do not blame you. I don't even blame him. There's so much to life than just loving a boy. So much that I'm looking ahead of me and I'm thrilled of the possibilities and opportunities life will throw my way. I never do freedom wall posts. But I just hope that maybe you'd come across this one and read it til the end. You're much younger than us, which means you have so much more ahead of you. I just feel maternal in some way when I found out. What I know is that you're strong and smart, maybe even smarter than both me and him. You could be so much better, and I truly believe you will. It was almost 3 years of love and firsts between him and I. And I've learned so much. I hope you grow to understand that I couldn't hate you even if I tried. I just wish to never see you again, even just a glimpse.

College feels so lonely. Here you are trying your best to put yourself out there and be friendly with people, but you can't seem to connect with people, as if there is an invisible barrier that prevents you. Talking to people here feels so transactional, and any attempt to start a meaningful conversation is met with awkward silence, and even if you try to maintain and initiate, there seems to be only emotional detachment on the other person. But when they are with their circles, they're suddenly a different person. College is already so overwhelming on its own, but it's made worse with the fact that every person you talk to treats you like some npc. I guess I'm still adjusting, but I think I should accept that I'll be alone for quite a long time.

A gentle and friendly REMINDER to all. [CAR] is a very small community compared to other constituents and the last thing we should be doing is fighting each other, spreading hates/rumors, and tearing each other down. Our freedom wall was meant to connect us, not divide us. I just want to address a few things, and these are only my opinions. On cheating issues and ellegations. To those who cheats even minor or grave offenses, please remember our shared value, as iskos ans iskas, HONOR before excellence. Your achievements would mean nothing if they were built through dishonesty. To those who expose cheaters publicly. Instead of publicly announcing, it is better to approach or report it directly to the respective instructors / professors. The matter or issue would be handled fairly and professionaly without unnecessary humiliation. Now we move on with issues regarding hate speeches and personal attacks. I know that we all have the freedom to express ourselves, but may this come with responsibility. Public shaming barely and rarely solves any problem, it just makes it worse. If you have issues with a particular someone, consider approaching them personally and privately, this creates a better space for communications and resolving the issue. This will give both parties sense of responsibility and reflection. I know for a fact that being anonymous could make us feel bold and untouchable, but remember that online words can trigger never ending cycles of retaliation. Let us use this space to lift and help each other. Share helpful ideas and insights. Build a community we can be proud of. We can always disgaree while being respectful. Our community should be built with mutual understandings, trusts, accountabilities, empathy, honor, and excellence. Let us choose to understand over hate, communication over shaming, and integrity over shortcuts.

less fw drama, more college shipping It started with a paper cut. Not a metaphorical one—CS would never allow that kind of narrative laziness—but a real, tangible, analog cut from a stack of printed records IM had been reviewing. CS saw it happen. The moment was inefficiently slow, like their internal processor had downclocked to witness it: IM flipping through sheets, one page a bit too sharp, the slight wince, the subtle recoil. Barely half a second, but CS's systems flagged it. Threat detected. Damage incurred. “Your finger is bleeding,” CS said flatly. IM looked up. “Yes, I noticed.” “Allow me.” They stood, crossed the room, and pulled open a drawer in IM’s filing cabinet—of course they knew where the first aid kit was. Of course they had logged it in their mental map of the office, under ‘Emergency Response Protocols’—just in case. Everything with CS had a ‘just in case.’ IM didn’t speak. He just watched as CS opened the alcohol pad, peeled the wrapper with robotic precision, and took IM’s hand with fingers that were steady, cold, and slightly trembling. Only slightly. The antiseptic sting made IM flinch. CS noticed. Logged it. Adjusted pressure. “You are careless with analog mediums,” CS said. “I will digitize your system next time.” IM smiled. “You’re worried.” CS didn’t answer. IM tilted his head. “You don’t have to be.” “I know.” But their hands lingered. And IM didn’t pull away. It became a pattern. Not injuries, but small, quiet moments like that. Human ones. Lunch deliveries CS never admitted were scheduled at times they knew IM skipped meals. USBs left on desks with spreadsheets already formatted to IM’s personal visual preferences. A post-it note IM once left that said, “Reminder: you’re allowed to rest." One evening, CS was in IM’s office again. Just... existing. They sat across from each other, both pretending they were reviewing papers, though neither of them had said a word in fifteen minutes. The rain tapped against the windows again—always rain in their story, always the soft hiss of static to accompany unspoken things. CS finally spoke. “My system has not crashed in 438 days.” IM looked up. “That’s good.” “It is statistically unusual. I have not maintained this level of performance before.” IM blinked, then smiled softly. “So you’re saying... I’m good for your system?” CS considered that. “Yes. Though it is disorienting.” IM stood up, crossed the room, and leaned over CS’s chair. He tapped the edge of their desk, slowly. “I could stay longer. Help optimize the system.” CS’s processes spiked. “You mean... you aren't leaving?” “I mean I could stay. Tonight.” A pause. CS didn’t speak. Just reached for IM’s hand, slow and cautious—like touching a variable mid-simulation—and held it. Confirmed.

📢 An Open Letter to All [CAR] Student-Leaders and Students First, I want to express my gratitude to all [CAR] student-leaders who dedicate their time and effort to serving the student body. You are, in many ways, the backbone of our university. However, this message comes with an honest appeal: stay grounded. Titles and positions do not place anyone above basic respect. Too often, I’ve seen certain student-leaders, especially those in or formerly part of the USC carrying themselves with arrogance, masking rudeness and belittling behavior behind “humor.” Each person has different limits. Constantly putting others down, acting as if you’re always superior, or turning every space into a stage for your dominance is not leadership—IT’S BULLYING. In classrooms, we must maintain a respectful teacher-student atmosphere. Yet, some student-leaders treat class like their personal territory, chatting with professors as if in private while others are learning, disrupting lessons, and overstepping boundaries. This affects both the quality of teaching and the comfort of your peers. We also need to talk about authenticity. Many present a polished, “for the people” image in public, but are passive-aggressive, entitled, and dismissive in smaller circles. If we can call out misconduct in the Philippine Senate, why not here in [CAR]? Toxic leadership, no matter the scale is still toxic. It’s no surprise that many students hesitate to join political orgs when the culture feels exclusive, performative, and unsafe. Just as we have a gender desk to address sexual misconduct, we also need concrete anti-bullying measures on campus because abuse of power doesn’t always wear the face we expect. Leadership is a responsibility, not a crown. [CAR] deserves leaders who uplift, not leaders who trample.

I made a Grade Calculator in Google Sheets that is designed to help UP students track their academic performance with ease and accuracy. 📌 Inspired by the '[NCR] GPA and Grade Calculator' excel sheet. The Grade Calculator has the following functions: - GWA Calculator (Simple weighted average for your grades) - Cumulative GWA Calculator (Stores and compounds grades from previous semesters) - Latin Honor Calculator (Shows the minimum grades needed in your remaining units to qualify for Latin honors) - Course Grade Calculator (Tracks scores in your subjects) To use the Grade Calculator, open the link below and just make a copy of the Google Sheet. Do not download -- Excel is not supported. If you have any suggestions or if you want to report any bugs go to 'Changelog' and under User Feedback Forms, you can find a Google Forms Link. ⚠️ This project is independently created and is not affiliated with or endorsed by the University of the Philippines.

SHEMPRE DI LNG DPAT CSS AND CAC MAY LOVE STORY CS had never been one for sentiment. Its world was one of logic gates, clean architectures, and neat binary outcomes. But after the Chancellor crisis, it had found itself... aware. Not just of CAC’s and CSS’s repaired bond, but of the tall, deliberate figure who had stood beside it in the council chamber: the [DEPARTMENT], cousin of CSS. IM was... infuriating. Too smooth, too strategic, too good at turning data into persuasion. Where CS was blunt—facts, results,—IM wrapped those same facts in silk and handed them over like a gift. And yet, in the quiet nights spent preparing their case, CS found itself recalculating the rhythm of IM’s speech, the way the edge of their voice dipped when they were serious, the faint scent of coffee and leather that lingered after they left. It was... distracting. Weeks later, the Chancellor’s shadow had receded, and the campus was beginning to breathe again. But CS kept finding reasons to see IM—consulting on budget models, “reviewing” reports that didn’t really need reviewing, running hypothetical simulations just to have an excuse to talk. One rainy afternoon, they found themselves alone in IM’s office. Outside, the city was blurred by water on the windows, and the air was heavy with that warm, electric stillness before a storm breaks. “You’ve been quiet,” IM said, glancing up from a set of quarterly projections. “I’ve been... processing,” CS replied. “Data suggests... my systems have been altered.” IM raised an eyebrow. “Altered?” “Yes,” CS said, the words clipped but certain. “Since working with you, I have observed inefficiencies in my processes. Distractions. Fluctuations in focus. Intermittent... spikes in output when you are present.” IM leaned back in their chair, a slow smile forming. “You’re describing attraction, CS. Not a system error.” CS tilted its head. “Attraction is... inefficient.” “Not always,” IM said, standing and crossing the space between them. “Sometimes it’s the catalyst for innovation. A force that pushes you to go beyond your default programming.” They stopped just in front of CS, close enough for the faint warmth of their presence to bleed through the cool chrome-and-ozone stillness of CS’s aura. “You’re not the only one who’s been... altered.” CS’s voice was low, precise. “Confirm.” IM’s answer was not in words. They reached out, fingers brushing the edge of CS’s polished arm—an intentional, calculated gesture that still sent a shock through CS’s circuits. “Confirmed.” It was not a grand declaration, not the chaotic fire of CAC and CSS’s reunion. It was quieter, more deliberate: a mutual acknowledgment between two minds that valued precision, yet found themselves willing to embrace the unpredictable variable of each other.

As a senior, I want to personally welcome all u freshies who choose Baguio as their campus and I know you'll definitely NOT regret your decision. I know some of you are already worried about what is to come and some of you already have a hint of what to expect. I have a cousin who is really excited to learn next week and she has been question bombing me so much about the community so I'll do my best to honestly say something about our campus. (and i know you'll be reading this too cousin) It is NOT a perfect University. there is always good and bad side but then again so is every college. [CAR] is the best you can get. just stay cool all the time and you'll be fine. If you're an introvert like me, then most of the time you wont be really bothered from toxicity and negativity. Some of this things are just really cheap rants. It is diverse. even as a senior I'm still suprise the amount of people from different regions coming together in one place. It's not homogenous unlike other colleges. You might want to meet them and eventually you'll realize that socializing with them in person is just a unique experience. There is like an entirely new range of personality. Sometimes you might have different feelings but that's part of trying to understand. (you'd also realize that about 85-90% of the student pop are not even from the cordillera which is crazy considering its location) You'd meet a lot of working students like me. Half the time we study and the half the time we [REDACTED_NAME]' minimum wage just to pay for living. You'd meet brave students too. I dont think there is not one college in baguio that can rival our ability to call out problems, unfairness, and injustice. You'd spend a lot of time inside hiking up stairs (hiking through 4-10 story buildings doesn't count). Idk why but i think this campus was designed to make us hike. Maybe to turn it into a habit. healthy but slight annoying when youre late. Has good events. You might want anticipate really exciting events like [REDACTED_NAME], [REDACTED_NAME] or org events Tuition is free so no worrying abt that.; There are good profs and terror prof that's all i can think for now. full take might end up being like a wattpad story. I'm sure other seniors might have a completely different opinion to give but in the end it's up to you how you view your experience.

It smelled of turpentine, old paper, and the electric hum of a live microphone. CAC was chaotic, beautiful, and felt everything intensely. Its oldest friend was the [DEPARTMENT]. CSS was a creature of order and analysis, its form a steady, humanoid shape of muted tweed that smelled of coffee and quiet libraries. Their third was the [DEPARTMENT]. CS was a being of clean lines and hard facts, manifesting as polished chrome and the faint scent of ozone. They were an unlikely, inseparable trio. “It’s an insult to language!” CAC wailed, its form a flurry of angry, scattered letters. “A student submitted a poem that is just the word ‘silence’ repeated a hundred times! They claim it’s ‘minimalist deconstruction.’ I call it lazy!” “A fascinating study in the semiotics of repetition,” CSS mused, its calm baritone a soothing balm. “It explores how context, not content, creates meaning. The impulse is quite profound.” “It’s inefficient communication,” CS stated, its voice a clear, clean tone. “Data redundancy. Hypothesis: failure. Q.E.D.” CSS would offer a small, fond smile. It loved CS’s brutal honesty, but its gaze always lingered on CAC. It found a strange peace in charting CAC’s moods, a comfort in its vibrant presence. For years, this was their friendship. Then, the Chancellor descended. It was not a being of bureaucracy, but of silence. A tall, sharp-suited silhouette whose face was a void of shadow, it radiated an aura of oppressive order that drained the colour from the air. Its decrees were not spoken; they simply appeared, cold and final. The latest was the most chilling: All resources will be redirected to programs with Quantifiable Impact. The words were a death sentence for CAC. Its vibrant light dimmed to a fragile, grey flicker. In the sterile meeting room, the Chancellor’s silent presence pressed down on them. CSS stood, its form rigid. It felt the Chancellor’s cold logic like a hand on its shoulder. To survive, it had to speak the same language. It had to be ruthless. “We must be guided by empirical reality,” CSS stated, its voice flat, avoiding CAC’s terrified shimmer. “We must protect the disciplines that provide hard data and testable theories. Science and the analysis of society are the bedrock of progress.” It paused, the silence in the room growing heavy. “The arts, communication, literature... they are subjective. Their impact is not quantifiable. They are, logically, a non-essential variable.” It wasn't just a betrayal. It was an echo of the Chancellor’s own chilling philosophy. CS remained impassive, but CAC made a sound, a quiet shatter like a heart breaking. And then the oppressive silence in the room snuffed it out, leaving only a cold spot where it had been. The days that followed were grey. The Chancellor’s influence bled across the campus, muting laughter and enforcing a grim, silent order. This was the world CSS’s logic had helped create. It was efficient. It was orderly. It was a graveyard. Then, the protest began. It started with a single, defiant voice from a student, then a poem wheat-pasted on a wall. It swelled into a roar. Students poured from every college, armed with placards painted in angry, vibrant colours. ANG SALITA AY SANDATA! (The Word is a Weapon!). PAG-ASA, HINDI PORSYENTO ([REDACTED_NAME], not Percentages). TUMINDIG, SINING! (Rise up, Art!). They were a chaotic, passionate, living wave of dissent crashing against the Chancellor's cold order. They were a social phenomenon. A living, breathing data set of human rebellion. CSS watched from a window as CS appeared beside it. “Your hypothesis was that CAC was non-essential,” CS stated. “The observable data—this protest—proves your hypothesis false. The variable you dismissed is the cause of this campus-wide systemic response. Your logic was flawed.” The clinical words struck CSS harder than any emotional plea. This wasn’t just a protest. This was CAC’s impact, made manifest in the raised fists, the furious poetry, the defiant broadcasts. This was the data it had refused to see. "I didn't just make a logical error," CSS whispered, the admission aching. "I was a coward. I let the Chancellor’s logic become my own." It found what was left of CAC in the empty theatre. A faint, trembling wisp of light hovered on the stage, so weak it was barely visible. “CAC?” CSS’s voice was soft, stripped of all analysis. The wisp flickered. A thought, thin as paper, echoed in the space. "Have you come to measure how little of me is left?" “No,” CSS said, walking to the edge of the stage. It gestured toward the distant sound of the chanting. “I came to present my findings. The Chancellor was wrong. And I was wrong to believe it. I tried to measure your value with my metrics, but the real proof of your worth is out there. That protest... that is your quantifiable impact. It is anger, and loyalty, and love. It’s the most powerful data on this campus.” It held out a hand. “A world without you isn’t logical. It’s just silent. Please.” The wisp of light trembled. "You said I didn't matter." “I was a fool,” CSS said, its voice thick with a feeling it couldn’t chart. “You are the only thing that does.” Slowly, the wisp drifted down and touched CSS’s outstretched hand. The contact was electric. CSS’s solid, grounding presence gave the chaos a form to cling to. CAC’s fragile light poured into CSS, filling its grey world with sudden, breathtaking colour. It grew brighter, stronger, coalescing into its familiar, shimmering form. CAC was whole again. It leaned against CSS, its chaotic energy settling into a warm, contented hum. CSS didn’t analyze the feeling. It just felt it, wrapping an arm around CAC’s shimmering form, holding it close. The next day, a joint proposal from CAC and CSS was submitted. As it landed on the Chancellor’s desk, the roar of the student protest outside the administration building rose to a crescendo. This was not a request. Backed by the voice of the people, it was a demand. The Chancellor, faced with a force it could not silence or quantify, relented. Weeks later, the three friends sat on their bench. CS was explaining a theory, but CAC was leaning its vibrant head against CSS’s shoulder. And CSS had an arm wrapped securely around CAC, its steady form a perfect contrast to the beautiful chaos it held. CS finished its explanation. “Interesting,” it stated, observing them. “The two of you have achieved a new, stable equilibrium.” And holding CAC closer as the distant sounds of music and poetry returned to the campus, CSS knew it was true. This was more than friendship. This was a complete, unassailable equation.

I'm chasing a ghost, I've tried dating but of all the people I've been going on dates with only three people have ever gone out of their way to be the ones to talk to me first, to be the ones to initiate conversation or ask after me, and for various reasons we just didn't work out, and that's okay. But... I'm starting to feel like this is just how it is, I'm not on anyone's top of mind, and I guess I'll always have to be the one chatting first, waiting for replies, or asking to go on dates. I found someone new, and I like them a lot, but it hurts me that they don't make the effort to chat first, but when they do reply, I feel relieved and happy to talk to them, until once again I'm left waiting, but when we finally meet up and go on dates its great, and yet, the waiting takes its toll on me, but I think this is how my love life is now, I suppose it would be foolish of me to expect I was worth so much attention, and so much time. I guess I'm lucky to be noticed at all, I spent so much time not being seen at all that I guess I can stomach this, I guess I can take this, at least there's a chance it will work out. So for now I'll chase a ghost, because the feeling of being wanted, even if I end up waiting so long, is something I don't wanna let go of right now. May we all be so lucky to feel wanted as often as we need it, but for now, I chase my ghost.

there’s a growing call among UP students to abolish the UPCAT, with the argument that it upholds inequality by favoring the privileged: those with access to review centers, better schools, and stable home environments. rhe anger behind this stance is real, but the strategy is flawed. UPCAT is not the enemy; it serves as the x-ray. it doesn’t create the gap — it reveals it. destroying the test won’t eliminate inequality; it’ll just blind us to where students actually stand. you don’t fix systemic injustice by killing the measuring tool. you fix the system that produces unequal preparation in the first place. what makes this worse is the hollow framing: “abolish first, fix later.” that’s not a strategy — that’s a tantrum. what replaces the UPCAT? grades? they’re inflated and inconsistent. recommendations? they favor the well-connected. a lottery? That’s randomness dressed as fairness. unless you have a concrete, superior system ready to replace it, removing the test will only make admissions less just, not more. let’s be clear, UP has limited resources. limited professors, classrooms, labs, among other things. it cannot admit everyone who wants in. a selection mechanism has to exist. and while imperfect, UPCAT is one of the few tools that still allows for underprivileged but high-potential students to break through on merit. take it away without a surgical alternative and you hand the gates right back to those who can afford inflated grades and padded transcripts. the call to abolish UPCAT is driven more by emotion than reform. if we truly care about equity, the fight isn’t at the gates of UP — it’s in the broken foundation of Philippine basic education. fund public schools. strengthen early education. fix the curriculum. train and pay teachers well. that’s where the real work begins. until then, abolishing UPCAT is just a performance, one that risks doing more harm than good. destruction isn’t progress. reform takes planning. and unless you’re ready to build from the roots up, don’t burn down the one thing still holding the line.

You’ve spoken frequently about being face-shamed. And to be clear, no one deserves that kind of treatment. It is unfair, and I will not take that from you. But let us not pretend that the discomfort people feel around you has anything to do with your appearance. It is your behavior. Your tendency to present yourself as principled, thoughtful, even morally superior, while consistently doing the opposite. You involve yourself in conversations that do not concern you. You speak with authority in spaces where your presence is no longer welcome. You change your tone depending on who is listening, and you call it intelligence. But it is not. It is cowardice in disguise. You are no longer in BS Math. You left, and that is your choice. But since then, you have spoken about the [DEPARTMENT], as if walking away has somehow made you better than those who stayed. You go out of your way to insult Computer Science students in particular, as if putting others down will rewrite your own history. It does not. And then there is your fixation on burgis — your disdain for students who, in your words, have money but attend a state university. You say this often. Loudly. As if you are some kind of exception. But by your own admission, you have spent thousands on video games. You are not criticizing a system. You are pretending to stand apart from it while fully benefiting from it. You say you hate performative people. You say you hate privilege. You say you hate hypocrisy. But the person you describe is you. That is why your presence in our batch is limited. The few who still gravitate toward you are seniors — people who simply have not spent enough time to see through the performance. Do better, R. Or at the very least, stop pretending to be something you are not. It should not take this many words to describe one person. And yet, here we are.

Tonight, I sat in our ordinary living room, in front of a regular electric fan. And I couldn't bring myself to move — because the wind reminded me of the Baguio air that once brushed against our skin as we sat outside your dorm, sharing that quiet stairway in the middle of the night. It was freezing back then. But I felt warm because you were there. Because your presence made me feel alive. I’ll probably never see you again. But I still thank God for every moment I had with you. And maybe, just maybe someday, when a wind like that brushes against your skin... you’ll think of me too. Of us.

I hated graduating. I hated seeing the situationship that crashed and burned. I hated the feeling of reality hitting that I no longer had any time with the people I truly cared about. I hated the regret of realizing I should’ve ended that relationship sooner. I hated being the trophy paraded around by family that I hadn’t talked to in years. I hated that I wasn’t able to cry for our friendship. I hated that I couldn’t put my medal on by myself. I hated the weight of my org pins whispering in my ears that I hadn’t done enough. I hated thinking how hollow this friendship was in those last few minutes. I hated that the only person that’s at fault for my grades was myself. I hated knowing that I’d never talk to you and get to know you more ever again. I hated laughing along with people I should have cut off. I hated myself for never being anything more than the +1. I hated that this was never about “me”, as I am, but the “me” that they wanted. I hated not being able to say goodbye. I hated the burden of those flowers that I never asked for. I hated that I wasn’t the one laughing and taking selfies with you. I hated looking at the people I had hurt and smiling like I was innocent. I hated walking up on that stage knowing that it was my last moment of being free. I hated that I’d have to live in that suffocating household again. I hated seeing the people I chose to be with, rather than the people I could’ve chosen to be with better. I hated that there will never be another “next week” to meet up again. More than anything else, I hate that I can never love this day that everyone else loves with so much bittersweetness.

How do I tell my mother (solo parent, housewife) that I can no longer provide for our family? She's been relying on me and my older sister to provide for our family, even far relatives. Since high-school, all our allowances from scholarships and cash prizes from school contests go to our mom. She simply buys us jollibee and then use it to pay for our bills and sometimes assist our orphaned pinsans. Even in college, all our DOST, SLAS and municipal scholarship, all of those go to her. From the 25k total of SLAS, I only get 2k, sometimes none at all because my mother says she supports us with our studies already. Even my sister who is now working, all her salary goes to my mom. Don't get me wrong, my mother gives me allowances a week, as well as allowances for my sister's lunch and transportation to work. These allowances did not come from my mother's pocket, she asks for it monthly from our grandmother and grandfather. But it's not enough, I'm always left with eating cup noodles multiple times a week as a meal because of the limited allowance given to me here in my dorm. I bet my sister struggles with this, too. Now, even in my graduation my mom expected me to save money from my weekly allowances to buy my own [REDACTED_NAME] and Sablay + Money for our fare. I tried talking to her about it but she gave me the cold shoulder. When I said that "[REDACTED_NAME], [REDACTED_NAME] [REDACTED_NAME] nagpo-provide sa [REDACTED_NAME] ate", she got mad and stopped cooking food for us at home. She stopped doing the house chores and didn't feed me and my sister for 3 days. My sister and I had to buy canned goods, noodles, and egg with our own money for those days. We'd buy her food too but she throws it away. She only forgave me when I gave her my last 5k from my SLAS that I tried to save (and wanted to keep a secret) for me to buy [REDACTED_NAME], new sandals, and my Sablay for graduation. I feel like a piggy bank, like I was born as an investment. Born to provide for my family regardless of my wishes. How can I address this matter? My sister and I can't leave our house because we have no savings. We can't save for our savings because our mom demands we give her my sister's salary and my allowances from scholarships. If we don't, she either cries and tries to harm herself saying that she failed as a mother or gives us the cold shoulder and bad mouths telling our relatives that my sister and I have become greedy and blinded by money. She also does not pay for the bills if we don't give her our money or provide proof that my scholarship allowances are indeed delayed (and I am not just trying to keep it for myself). I tried reaching out to other relatives, but they are all on our mother's side. P.S. I'm not from [CAR]. I just wanted to send it here because my mom might see it if I send it on our campus' freedom wall.

All you leftists angry over the wage hike bill, you have no idea how basic economics work. My family's small business is running on razor thin margins, it's a stroke of luck that we even get good months where we profit. If that bill passed we'll either be forced to increase prices, contributing to the inflation that you all hate so much, or forced to dismiss one or two of our employees to afford everyone else's increased wages, turning them into unemployment statistics, which you also hate. If we don't do either, the business will eventually be forced to close, so we and all our employees will lose our jobs making an even worse unemployment statistic. We're celebrating that this bill failed to pass, and I am appalled at seeing all these posts coming from people who hate capitalism so much, but don't even know how capitalism works. Isn't it common sense to know your enemy to fight them properly?
